speaker: interviewer
text: --is Khane Baltser. You spoke about your life in the orphanage. Do you know who sponsored, who took care of that orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- the same people that was in ghetto. And these same people help [INAUDIBLE] survived from ghetto. They have special teachers was survived from ghetto. We have special doctors what survived from ghetto. They was take care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said that you had a school. Was it a regular school? Or was it just a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a regular Jewish school. We was lost three years. We was older than they teach us, but we have different kids-- oldest and youngest, because they teach us the same-- the same what we can-- what we can understand to know by the-- to reading in Yiddish and in talking-- writing in Yiddish. But we was kids. We know a lot of Yiddish. And we learning [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] geography, history. We're talking about ghetto. We're talking about the war. We're talking about everything. This was, like-- but we're talking only in Yiddish, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened, meanwhile, to your sister while you were in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- no. I did not know about my sister. But after two months, my sister find out that I was in life, because when they opened the orphanage for the kids, they do a special-- they-- they're-- they're writing this-- the names for us. And they put took this in the newspaper. And they send this-- that to Poland, because people, when they came back to Lithuania, to Kovno, they was look out who is-- who survived. And my sister, she saw my name, what I was survive. And she came to Kovno to-- to be together again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened afterward? How-- what-- when-- after you met with your sister, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, when I met my sister, she was-- she was alone, because she don't know about her husband. And my-- and she helped my sister, because she was came from the partisan. And she gave her a part-- an apartment. And she can't-- my sister, she can't work, because she was-- she was sick when the Germans, they come-- they killed-- they killed her baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we was see each other, but she can't take me home because she did not work. And in this place where I was, they take care of us. We have to eat, we have clothes, it was warm, it was-- we have everything, like a home.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you stayed in that home for two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened afterwards? What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was start-- try to learning much more. And I find out a job, bookkeeper. And I was special go to a school to learning to pass the-- the-- the-- the bookkeeper lessons. And later on, they live together-- near together, but in-- in the same place with my sister. In the same-- in the same building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your life continue afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: After, I was married. This was in 1954. And my husband, he was born in Moscow, and he took me to Moscow. And in 1956, I was born my first baby. In 1958, I was born my other baby. Two daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband-- my husband's name was Boris Baltser. My daughter's oldest name is Irina Baltser. My youngest daughter is-- was Marina Baltser.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Moscow?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Moscow 30, 40 years. And after, in 1988-- before. This was in 198-- in the 1980s. I was start to-- to go to-- moved from Russia. And I talked to my sister. And I need help. And she helped me, and she give me special papers, the-- the-- the-- to go out from Russia with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when you left Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to United States. This was in 1988.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who went with you? Who came with you to United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My two daughters and my daughters' two husband-- the husbands. And two grandchildren. Everybody have one-- one kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I'm retired. And I've been-- I am happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good. Now, you said that you were singing songs in the ghetto. Do you remember any of the songs?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a lot of songs from ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you maybe sing one?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a song was-- the peoples, they give everything. They give the gold. They give everything to be in life. [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] This was the life in ghetto. The words what we have in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. And thank you--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this picture-- when I was in the orphanage, they take pictures for us. And I was sing songs in Yiddish. This is my uncle, Lusi Shamoilov, before the war. And this is my uncle, Lusi Shamoilov, when the Germans, they killed him. He was in the partisan company, and they grabbed them and they killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- the-- the picture from the orphanage. In the white blouse, it's me. This is a picture, I was 19. I was working, and I felt very good. This picture was in 1988, before I left Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the picture was my two daughters, Marina and Irina, and them's friend. This picture, we celebrated my son-in-law's birthday. He was 40. My daughter and my granddaughter is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marina and Dena and Alexander. This is my oldest daughter, Irina, in American. This picture is my grandchildren, Michael and Dena. Michael was 15, and Dena was 16. This is a concert to benefit for new people what came to American.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're singing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was singing Yiddish songs.
